title: Quick check: EBS, instance store, EFS

questions.prompt: When should you use instance store (the local NVMe SSDs on certain EC2 instance types) vs. EBS?

choices.text: Use instance store for ephemeral, performance-critical scratch data and EBS for anything that must survive an instance stop or failure. Instance store sits physically on the host machine — very fast (GB/s sequential, hundreds of thousands of IOPS) and free (included with the instance), but the data is lost the moment the instance stops, terminates, or the underlying hardware fails. This makes it ideal for: distributed databases with their own replication (Cassandra, Aerospike), intermediate scratch data in big-data pipelines (Spark shuffle), local caches, build/CI workspaces. EBS persists across instance lifecycle events and is the right choice for application data, root volumes for stateful workloads, databases that don't have built-in cross-node replication, and anywhere data loss would be costly. The mistake to avoid: putting persistent application data on instance store and discovering after the first instance failure that you've lost it.

questions.explanation: Instance store is ephemeral by design. Free and fast, but the data is gone on failure. Use it where ephemerality is fine; never where data loss matters.

questions.prompt: A web app cluster of 10 EC2 instances needs to serve user-uploaded images. The team considers EFS for shared file storage. Better option?

choices.text: Use S3 + CloudFront, not EFS. EFS gives you a shared file system that all 10 instances can mount, which solves the synchronization problem but at significant ongoing cost (~$0.30/GB/month vs S3 Standard's $0.023/GB/month, plus EFS file-operation overhead) and unnecessary application complexity (mount points, NFS tuning, file permissions). The cleaner architecture: uploads go directly to S3 via pre-signed URLs, images are served from CloudFront (cached at edge for low latency), the application stores S3 keys in the database. No file-system semantics needed, scales infinitely, costs a fraction, integrates with CDN naturally. EFS is correct when the application genuinely needs file-system semantics — POSIX operations, file locking, applications that can't be modified to use object storage. For greenfield web apps storing user uploads, S3 is almost always the right choice.

questions.explanation: Default to S3 for shared, immutable, web-accessible content. EFS is for legacy apps that need file-system semantics or specific POSIX behavior. The cost and complexity difference is significant.
